name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) Management Protocol
description: A comprehensive, evidence-based protocol for the diagnosis and management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) in primary care settings, adhering to the 2022 ACG Clinical Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of GERD.

templateData.duration: Initial treatment phase: 8 weeks. Ongoing management: As needed, based on symptom control and clinical assessment.
goals: Achieve complete or significant resolution of typical GERD symptoms (e.g., heartburn, regurgitation) within 8 weeks.
Prevent complications of GERD such as erosive esophagitis, stricture, or Barrett's esophagus.
Improve patient's quality of life related to GERD symptoms.
Minimize reliance on pharmacotherapy through lifestyle modifications.
Identify and address atypical GERD symptoms or alarm features requiring further investigation.

items: Trial of empiric PPI therapy for typical GERD symptoms.
Consider upper endoscopy for alarm symptoms (dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, weight loss, iron deficiency anemia) or failure of empiric PPI therapy.
Consider ambulatory reflux monitoring (pH or impedance-pH) for refractory symptoms despite PPIs or atypical symptoms.
Initiate Proton Pump Inhibitor (PPI) therapy once daily, 30-60 minutes before the first meal.
If symptoms persist, consider increasing PPI to twice daily.
Consider H2 Receptor Antagonists (H2RAs) for on-demand symptom relief or as an adjunct to PPIs, particularly for nocturnal symptoms.
Evaluate need for step-down therapy after 8 weeks of successful PPI treatment.
Educate on the chronic nature of GERD and the importance of adherence to lifestyle and medication regimens.
Discuss potential side effects of medications and strategies for mitigation.
Provide guidance on dietary triggers and portion control.
Counsel on when to seek immediate medical attention (e.g., severe chest pain, choking, persistent dysphagia).
Referral to gastroenterologist for endoscopy, advanced diagnostics (e.g., manometry, impedance-pH monitoring), or consideration of surgical options (e.g., fundoplication) for refractory GERD.
Consider adding alginates or antacids for breakthrough symptoms.

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Pantoprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Esomeprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Famotidine (H2RA)
medications.frequency: Once or twice daily, or as needed
medications.instructions: For on-demand symptom relief, particularly nocturnal symptoms, or as an adjunct to PPIs. Take before meals or at bedtime.

lifestyle: Elevate the head of the bed by 6-8 inches using blocks or a wedge pillow (not extra pillows).
Avoid lying down within 2-3 hours after eating.
Identify and avoid individual trigger foods (e.g., fatty foods, spicy foods, chocolate, caffeine, alcohol, peppermint, citrus).
Eat smaller, more frequent meals instead of large ones.
Achieve and maintain a healthy weight (if overweight or obese).
Avoid tight-fitting clothing around the abdomen.

followUp.frequency: Initial follow-up in 4-8 weeks after starting therapy. Subsequent follow-up every 6-12 months for chronic management or as needed for symptom changes.
metrics: Symptom frequency and severity (e.g., heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia).
Adherence to medication and lifestyle modifications.
Presence of new or worsening alarm symptoms.

warnings: Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, weight loss, iron deficiency anemia) warrant prompt endoscopy, not just empiric therapy.

Long-term PPI use has been associated with potential risks including C. difficile infection, pneumonia, bone fractures, and kidney disease; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration necessary.
PPIs should be taken 30-60 minutes before the first meal for optimal efficacy.
Do not abruptly discontinue PPIs after prolonged use due to potential rebound acid hypersecretion.
GERD symptoms can mimic cardiac chest pain; rule out cardiac causes in patients presenting with chest pain.
Patients with Barrett's esophagus require endoscopic surveillance.
Consider drug interactions with PPIs (e.g., clopidogrel, methotrexate).
Referral to a specialist is indicated for refractory symptoms, alarm features, or complicated GERD.
